title: Depth never threaded into child tool-dispatch context — supervisor max_depth=2 grandchild level unreachable; depth-cap + depth-prune dead under live wiring
proof:
CONFIRMED REAL in current code. Chain verified end-to-end:

(1) agent_servicer.py:1967-1974 — _dispatch_builtin rebuilds parent_ctx for EVERY tool call with depth hardcoded to 0, keyed off the PARENT's start.session_key:
    `tenant_id = start.session_key.split("::")[0] if start.session_key else "default"`
    `parent_ctx = ParentContext(tenant_id=tenant_id or "default", parent_agent_id=start.model or "agent", parent_session_key=start.session_key or "session", depth=0, trace_id=start.session_key or "")`

(2) agent_servicer.py:1937-1942 — the child's tool executor reuses the PARENT's start frame:
    `async def _execute(child_event: ToolCallEvent) -> str:`
    `    if child_event.tool in _SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS: return json.dumps({"error": "subagent_no_recursive_spawn"})`
    `    return await self._dispatch_builtin(child_event, start, provider, file_state)`
So a child's tool call dispatches through the parent's depth-0 context; depth is never +1'd for the child.

(3) The executor is passed UNCHANGED down the whole child path: agent_servicer.py:2062-2064 `tool_executor=self._make_child_tool_executor(start, provider, file_state)` → tool_wrapper.py:584 `tool_executor=tool_executor` into run_child → runner.py:358 into _drive_and_collect → runner.py:419 into _drain_events → runner.py:652 `result_json = await _execute_child_tool(tool_executor, event)`. It is never re-bound to the child's child_ctx (depth+1, child session_key). Verified no other ParentContext construction with depth>0 exists in production (only agent_servicer.py:1968 depth=0; api.py:213 is inside child_context()'s own definition).

(4) The hard-reject at agent_servicer.py:1938-1939 papers over (2)/(3): any spawn tool from a child returns subagent_no_recursive_spawn, so a grandchild can never be created via the wrong-depth path.

(5) runner.py:822-825 depth-prune: `if child_depth >= max_depth - 1:` discards subagent_spawn/spawn_many/spawn_inline. With max_depth=2 (supervisor.py:104, types.py:50, api.py:194) the FIRST child has child_ctx.depth=1 (child_context does `depth=min(self.depth + 1, 255)`, api.py:217), and `1 >= 2-1` is True — so spawn tools are stripped from the first child's schema already.

(6) supervisor.py:331 `if parent_ctx.depth >= self._policy.max_depth:` (max_depth=2). Because the only live parent_ctx is depth=0 and children's tool dispatch reuses that depth-0 ctx, this branch and the DEPTH_CAPPED emit at supervisor.py:332-333 never fire with depth>0 in production. The documented contract "parent → child → grandchild" (types.py:48-50, api.py:193) is unreachable; the live ceiling is depth 1 (parent → child only).

Net: defect is real. It is NOT a containment/security hole (children are correctly capped at depth 1 by TWO independent mechanisms — schema prune AND hard reject), so I downgrade severity from high to medium: it is a parity / dead-code / doc-vs-reality mismatch, not a runtime fault or escape.
refined_fix:
Pick one of two coherent paths.

Option A (make the documented contract real — single-level-or-deeper nesting): At spawn time, capture the child's ParentContext (depth+1, child session_key/agent_id, tenant) and build a child-scoped tool executor instead of reusing the parent's start at depth 0. Concretely: change `_make_child_tool_executor` (agent_servicer.py:1911-1944) to accept the child's `ParentContext` (or session_key+depth) and have `_dispatch_builtin` accept an optional `parent_ctx_override` rather than always rebuilding `depth=0` at agent_servicer.py:1968-1974. Then drop the `subagent_no_recursive_spawn` hard reject (agent_servicer.py:1938-1939) and let supervisor.py:331 + runner.py:822 do the real gating. The dispatcher already derives child_ctx via parent_ctx.child_context (tool_wrapper.py:553), so the child's ctx is available to thread.

Option B (accept single-level nesting as the product limit — least code, removes dead paths): Set DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH=1 (types.py:50, api.py:194) and SupervisorPolicy.max_depth=1 (supervisor.py:104), update the "parent → child → grandchild" docstrings to "parent → child", and either keep the hard reject as documented belt-and-braces or delete the now-redundant grandchild handling. This makes the documented contract match the live ceiling (depth 1) with no behavioral change.

Recommend Option B unless grandchild delegation is an actual product requirement — it removes the dead depth-cap/grandchild paths and aligns docs with reality at near-zero risk.

original.title: Depth never threaded into parent_ctx — supervisor max_depth=2 (grandchild) is unreachable; depth-cap + depth-prune are dead under live wiring
original.evidence: In `_dispatch_builtin` the parent context is rebuilt for EVERY tool call with a hardcoded depth: `parent_ctx = ParentContext(... depth=0 ...)` (agent_servicer.py:1972). A spawned child's own tool calls run through `_make_child_tool_executor._execute` which calls `self._dispatch_builtin(child_event, start, ...)` reusing the PARENT's `start` frame — so a hypothetical grandchild spawn would again derive `depth=0` and key the per-parent counter on the parent's session_key, not the child's. To paper over this, `_make_child_tool_executor` hard-rejects all spawn tools from any child: `if child_event.tool in _SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS: return json.dumps({"error": "subagent_no_recursive_spawn"})` (agent_servicer.py:1938-1939). Net effect: the supervisor's `if parent_ctx.depth >= self._policy.max_depth` (supervisor.py:331) NEVER sees depth>0 in production, and the runner's depth self-prune `if child_depth >= max_depth - 1` (runner.py:822) prunes spawn tools from the FIRST child (depth 1 >= 2-1). The documented `max_depth=2` (DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH, types.py:50 — 'parent → child → grandchild') is therefore unreachable; the live ceiling is depth 1 (parent → child only). The depth-cap branch in try_acquire and the supervisor's depth machinery are effectively dead code under the real wiring.
original.claude_code_parity: Claude Code's Task tool: subagents cannot themselves spawn subagents (effective depth 1). This system advertises max_depth=2 and ships a depth-cap + depth self-prune that imply two-level nesting, but the wiring silently caps at 1 via a hard-reject. So behavior matches Claude Code's *limit* by accident, while the system's own stated contract (depth 2) is violated and the depth machinery is non-functional. A child near the ceiling does not 'lose the ability to spawn' through depth accounting — it is blanket-blocked one level too early.
original.proposed_fix: Thread real depth + the child's own session/agent identity into the child's tool-execution context. Capture the child `ParentContext` (depth+1, child session_key/agent_id) at spawn time and have `_make_child_tool_executor` build a child-scoped `parent_ctx` (instead of reusing `start` and rebuilding at depth 0), then let the supervisor's depth-cap and the runner's depth-prune do the gating. If single-level nesting is the intended product limit, set DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH/SupervisorPolicy.max_depth=1 and delete the unreachable grandchild paths + the `subagent_no_recursive_spawn` hard-reject so the documented contract matches reality.

title: Background subagent dispatcher constructed with an always-raising placeholder factory, never threaded into the tool path; referenced replace_factory() does not exist
proof:
All four sub-claims confirmed in current source.

(1) Placeholder factory unconditionally raises — entrypoint.py:1874-1885:
  async def _unwired_run_child_factory(req: SubagentRequest) -> Any:
      raise RuntimeError(
          "subagent run_child factory not wired; "
          "W1.1 must install one via "
          "AdminState.subagent_dispatcher.replace_factory()")
and it is the factory actually installed at line 1896-1901: AsyncSubagentDispatcher(store=..., run_child_factory=_unwired_run_child_factory, ...).

(2) replace_factory() does not exist anywhere. `grep -rn replace_factory --include=*.py python/` returns exactly ONE hit: the error string at entrypoint.py:1884. AsyncSubagentDispatcher.__slots__ (dispatcher.py:159-167) is ("_audit_log","_journal","_lock","_max_concurrent_per_tenant","_run_child_factory","_store","_tasks") — no replace_factory method on the class.

(3) The LLM tool-call site never threads the dispatcher. dispatch_subagent_spawn signature (tool_wrapper.py:300): `subagent_dispatcher: Any | None = None`. The agent_servicer call (agent_servicer.py:2045-2068) passes args_json/parent_ctx/agent_registry/provider/...parent_session_key but NOT subagent_dispatcher=. `grep subagent_dispatcher agent_servicer.py` returns zero hits; agent_servicer.py never imports AsyncSubagentDispatcher nor reads app.state.corlinman_subagent_dispatcher. So at the tool-call site subagent_dispatcher is always None → tool_wrapper.py:392-405 returns the clean BACKGROUND_NOT_IMPLEMENTED_ERROR ("run_in_background_not_implemented") rejection and _dispatch_via_background (which calls subagent_dispatcher.dispatch_async at tool_wrapper.py:1014) is unreachable.

(4) The dispatcher is otherwise dead. The only other consumer, routes_admin_b/subagents.py, calls only store.list_active/list_all/get and dispatcher.kill — there is NO route that calls dispatch_async. So dispatch_async / _run / _unwired_run_child_factory are never reached in production; the constructed dispatcher only ever serves read/kill. The dispatcher.py module docstring (lines 12-13) still claims it 'Spawn[s] an asyncio.create_task that invokes Supervisor.spawn_child_to_result' which never happens. Note: severity is medium (latent dead-wiring + stale docstring), not a live crash — the model path returns a typed rejection, never the RuntimeError.
refined_fix:
Two viable directions, both minimal.

(b) De-risk now (recommended, smallest): stop claiming a capability that does not exist.
  - entrypoint.py:1881-1885: change the placeholder to raise NotImplementedError with a message that does NOT reference a nonexistent replace_factory() (e.g. "background subagent dispatch not wired on this build"). The dispatcher's _run already folds it into a 'failed' row, but since no path schedules a task this is just to stop the dangling API reference.
  - dispatcher.py:12-13 (and 5-16): correct the module docstring to state the dispatcher is wired for read/kill + admin surface only and that the run_child_factory is supplied at construction (there is no replace_factory); drop the 'spawns asyncio.create_task that invokes Supervisor.spawn_child_to_result' claim until a real factory is installed.

(a) Finish the wiring (if background spawn is actually wanted):
  - Add AsyncSubagentDispatcher.replace_factory(self, factory: RunChildFactory) -> None (add "_run_child_factory" is already a slot; just `self._run_child_factory = factory`).
  - In agent_servicer.py:2045-2068 (and the spawn_many path at 2080-2097), thread subagent_dispatcher=<the app.state.corlinman_subagent_dispatcher handle> into dispatch_subagent_spawn / dispatch_subagent_spawn_many. The servicer would need a reference to it (inject via CorlinmanAgentServicer constructor in main.py:323/331 or read off app.state).
  - Replace _unwired_run_child_factory with a real supervisor-bound factory (closing over the Supervisor + agent registry + provider + tool_executor) so dispatch_async actually drives Supervisor.spawn_child_to_result as the docstring promises.

original.title: Background subagent dispatcher is constructed with a factory that always raises and is never threaded into the tool path; replace_factory() is referenced but does not exist
original.evidence: The gateway builds the dispatcher with a placeholder factory that unconditionally raises: `async def _unwired_run_child_factory(req): ... raise RuntimeError('subagent run_child factory not wired; W1.1 must install one via AdminState.subagent_dispatcher.replace_factory()')` (entrypoint.py:1874-1885). No `replace_factory` method exists anywhere — `grep replace_factory` returns only that error string, and `AsyncSubagentDispatcher.__slots__` (dispatcher.py:159-167) has no such method. Separately, `agent_servicer._dispatch_builtin` calls `dispatch_subagent_spawn(...)` WITHOUT passing `subagent_dispatcher=` (agent_servicer.py:2045-2068), so at the LLM tool-call site `subagent_dispatcher` is always `None` → `dispatch_subagent_spawn` returns the clean `run_in_background_not_implemented` rejection (tool_wrapper.py:392-405). So `_dispatch_via_background`/`dispatch_async`/the unwired factory are unreachable from the model. The dispatcher.py module docstring still claims it 'Spawn[s] an asyncio.create_task that invokes Supervisor.spawn_child_to_result' (dispatcher.py:13) which never happens — the constructed dispatcher is dead, and any future call to `dispatch_async` would create a background task that immediately fails with RuntimeError and writes a `failed` row.
original.claude_code_parity: R4-F3 holds at the model boundary: `run_in_background=true` cleanly returns `run_in_background_not_implemented`, matching the prior audit claim. The divergence is internal: a half-wired async-dispatch subsystem is constructed and published onto AdminState (so /admin/subagents lists it as 'available') but cannot deliver any background dispatch; the wiring instructions reference a `replace_factory()` method that was never implemented.
original.proposed_fix: Either (a) finish the wiring: add `AsyncSubagentDispatcher.replace_factory(factory)`, install a real supervisor-bound factory at boot, and thread `subagent_dispatcher=self._subagent_dispatcher` into the `dispatch_subagent_spawn` calls in agent_servicer; or (b) until then, do not construct/publish the dispatcher (or guard the `_unwired_run_child_factory` path behind an explicit 'not implemented' that never schedules a task) and correct the dispatcher.py module docstring to stop claiming it drives `spawn_child_to_result`.

title: Supervisor docstring claims an asyncio.Lock guards the counter RMW, but the lock is never acquired (dead code; correct only because the path is await-free)
proof:
`grep -n "_lock\|async with\|asyncio.Lock"` over the whole file returns exactly three hits: line 34 (docstring), line 223 (`__slots__` entry "_lock"), line 275 (`self._lock = asyncio.Lock()`). There is NO `async with self._lock` anywhere — the lock is created and never acquired.

The class docstring (lines 34-38) asserts the false invariant verbatim: "The supervisor uses :class:`asyncio.Lock` around the counter / read-modify-write so two concurrent ``try_acquire`` calls on the same / parent see consistent counts." The __init__ comment (lines 271-274) reinforces it: "Single asyncio lock guards both counter dicts."

`try_acquire` (lines 316-353) is fully synchronous (`def try_acquire(self, parent_ctx) -> Slot:`, no `await`) and does the read at line 339 `cur_parent = self._per_parent.get(parent_key, 0)` then the write at line 343 `self._per_parent[parent_key] = cur_parent + 1`, and again read line 346 / write line 351 for the tenant counter — never taking the lock. The release path (`Slot.release` lines 169-175 calling `_dec_parent` 298-305 / `_dec_tenant` 307-312) is likewise sync and lock-free. The RMW is atomic ONLY because no `await` separates read and write on a single-threaded loop, exactly as the finding states.

Severity-low confirmed: no caller drives the supervisor across threads. `grep -rn "run_coroutine_threadsafe|to_thread|ThreadPool|run_in_executor"` over the subagent dispatch paths returns nothing; the server dispatcher (dispatcher.py:317) schedules work via `asyncio.create_task` inside the loop. So the lock-free RMW is correct under current usage; the defect is the misleading dead lock plus a docstring guaranteeing an invariant the code does not actually enforce.
refined_fix:
Two valid options; the documentation-only fix is the minimal, lowest-risk one and matches current single-threaded reality:

OPTION A (minimal — drop the dead lock + correct the claim):
1. supervisor.py:275 — delete `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()` and remove the comment at 271-274.
2. supervisor.py:223 — remove `"_lock",` from `__slots__`.
3. supervisor.py:34-38 — replace the false invariant with an accurate one, e.g.: "try_acquire's counter read-modify-write is atomic by virtue of being await-free on a single-threaded asyncio loop (no yield point separates the read from the write), so no explicit lock is needed; this mirrors the Rust DashMap per-key entry guard." Also drop `import asyncio`? No — asyncio is still used elsewhere (wait_for, create_task), keep it.

OPTION B (defensive — only if the supervisor might ever be driven cross-thread): make the lock real. This requires making the RMW async, which is invasive because `try_acquire` is documented sync and `Slot.release`/`__del__` cannot be async. Prefer Option A unless a threaded caller is introduced. If kept lock-free, do NOT claim a lock-backed guarantee in the docstring.

original.title: Supervisor docstrings claim an asyncio.Lock guards the counter read-modify-write, but try_acquire never acquires it (correct only because the path is await-free)
original.evidence: The class docstring asserts: 'The supervisor uses asyncio.Lock around the counter read-modify-write so two concurrent try_acquire calls on the same parent see consistent counts' (supervisor.py:34-38), and `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()` is created (line 275) with a comment 'Single asyncio lock guards both counter dicts' (271-274). But `try_acquire` (316-353) is fully synchronous and NEVER does `async with self._lock` — the read (`cur_parent = self._per_parent.get(...)`) and write (`self._per_parent[parent_key] = cur_parent + 1`) at lines 339-343/346-351 are correct purely because no `await` separates them on a single-threaded loop. `_dec_parent`/`_dec_tenant` and `Slot.release` are likewise lock-free. The lock is dead code and the documented invariant is false.
original.claude_code_parity: N/A — internal consistency issue.
original.proposed_fix: Drop the misleading lock + docstring claims, OR (safer) actually take the lock if there is any chance the supervisor is driven across threads (e.g. via `loop.run_coroutine_threadsafe` or a thread-pool-backed caller) — in which case the current lock-free read-modify-write is a genuine race. As written under pure single-threaded asyncio it is correct, so demote the claim to 'atomic by virtue of being await-free' to avoid implying a guarantee the code does not provide.

title: max_wall_seconds default aliases the ceiling (60==60); stale docstrings advertise a non-existent 300s ceiling
proof:
Single constant aliased for BOTH default and ceiling:
- types.py:41 `DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS: int = 60`
- types.py:123 `max_wall_seconds: int = DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS` (TaskSpec per-task default = 60)
- tool_wrapper.py:871 `max_wall_seconds = raw.get("max_wall_seconds", DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS)` (LLM-facing arg parser default = 60 when omitted)
- supervisor.py:109 `max_wall_seconds_ceiling: int = DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS` (policy ceiling = 60)

Only production construction has NO override, so ceiling resolves to 60:
- agent_servicer.py:2629 `sup = Supervisor(SupervisorPolicy())` (grep confirms this and supervisor.py:258 are the only two `SupervisorPolicy(` call sites outside tests; neither passes max_wall_seconds_ceiling)

Clamp therefore never engages on default path:
- tool_wrapper.py:518-521 `if (max_wall_seconds_ceiling is not None and spec.max_wall_seconds > max_wall_seconds_ceiling):` → `60 > 60` = False. The ceiling provides zero headroom over the default; it only engages if the LLM explicitly requests >60s.

Stale docstrings claim a 300 ceiling that does not exist in code:
- tool_wrapper.py:347-348 "design's `[subagent].max_wall_seconds_ceiling` (default 300) is enforced from above"
- runner.py:428 "`max_wall_seconds_ceiling` (default 300 — see config block)"
- grep for `300` across corlinman-subagent/src and corlinman-agent/src/.../subagent/ returns ONLY these two docstring lines; no `300` literal exists. The supervisor docstring itself (supervisor.py:97) correctly says `max_wall_seconds_ceiling=60`, directly contradicting the runner/tool_wrapper docstrings.
refined_fix:
Two decisions, both small and mechanical:

(A) Decouple the constants so default and ceiling can diverge. In types.py add alongside line 41:
  `DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS: int = 60` (keep as per-task default)
  add `DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS_CEILING: int = 300` (new, for the policy upper bound)
Then change supervisor.py:109 `max_wall_seconds_ceiling: int = DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS` -> `= DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS_CEILING`, and update the docstring supervisor.py:97 (`=60` -> `=300`). This makes the clamp at tool_wrapper.py:518-525 actually provide headroom: a child defaults to 60s but the LLM may request up to 300s, which is what the docstrings already promise.

(B) If 60 was genuinely intended as the hard ceiling (no child should ever exceed 60s), instead leave supervisor.py:109 as-is and FIX the two lying docstrings: tool_wrapper.py:347-348 and runner.py:428 — change "default 300" to "default 60" to match supervisor.py:97 and the actual resolved value.

Recommended: option (A) — the supervisor.py comment block, runner.py, and tool_wrapper.py all describe an "enforced from above" ceiling that lets the LLM ask for more, which is meaningless when ceiling==default; 300 was clearly the design intent per `[subagent]` config references. Minimal diff: one new constant in types.py:41-area, one reference change at supervisor.py:109, one docstring fix at supervisor.py:97.

original.title: max_wall_seconds default equals the ceiling (60==60) and stale docstrings advertise a 300s ceiling that does not exist
original.evidence: `DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS = 60` (types.py:41) is used BOTH as the per-task default (`TaskSpec.max_wall_seconds: int = DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS`, types.py:123) AND as the policy ceiling (`SupervisorPolicy.max_wall_seconds_ceiling: int = DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS`, supervisor.py:109). The foreground clamp `if ... spec.max_wall_seconds > max_wall_seconds_ceiling` (tool_wrapper.py:518-521) is thus `60 > 60` = False on the default path, so the ceiling provides zero headroom and never engages unless the LLM explicitly asks for >60s. Meanwhile multiple docstrings claim the ceiling default is 300: tool_wrapper.py:347-348 ('[subagent].max_wall_seconds_ceiling (default 300)'), runner.py:428-432 ('default 300 — see config block'). No 300 value exists in code (`SupervisorPolicy.max_wall_seconds_ceiling` resolves to 60).
original.claude_code_parity: N/A — not a Claude Code semantic, but the stale 300s docs will mislead operators about the actual wall-clock budget a subagent gets.
original.proposed_fix: Decide the intended ceiling: if 300 was intended, set `SupervisorPolicy.max_wall_seconds_ceiling = 300` (decoupled from DEFAULT_MAX_WALL_SECONDS) so a child can actually run longer than the 60s default when requested; otherwise fix the docstrings in tool_wrapper.py and runner.py to say 60. Also decouple the per-task default constant from the ceiling constant so the two can diverge without aliasing.

title: Child tool-allowlist is advisory only: execution boundary runs any tool the parent holds, bypassing the runner's filtered subset
lines: runner.py:266-295 (filter→schema only), 372-423 + 624-661 (drain executes unchecked); agent_servicer.py:1937-1944 (executor only blocks spawn tools)
proof:
Confirmed in current source. The resolved allowlist is computed but used ONLY for the advertised schema, never for execution gating.

(1) runner.py:266-295 — `child_tool_names = _filter_tools_for_child(...)` (the parent∩card∩caller frozenset) is consumed only at line 295: `child_tools = _project_tool_schemas(parent_tools, child_tool_names)`. The frozenset itself is never passed further.

(2) runner.py:351-361 — run_child calls `_drive_and_collect(loop, chat_start, child_ctx, started_ms, task, provider=..., tool_executor=tool_executor, ...)` — `child_tool_names` is NOT among the args. _drive_and_collect (signature 372-383) likewise has no allowlist param and forwards only `tool_executor=tool_executor` to `_drain_events` (412-423).

(3) runner.py:624-661 — in `_drain_events`, for every ToolCallEvent: `result_json = await _execute_child_tool(tool_executor, event)` then `loop.feed_tool_result(...)`. The only guards are `tool_executor is None` (legacy) and `max_tool_calls` (cost budget). There is NO `if event.tool not in child_tool_names` check — any tool the child model emits is executed.

(4) ChildToolExecutor type (runner.py:70): `Callable[[ToolCallEvent], Awaitable[str]]` — no allowlist parameter exists in the contract.

(5) agent_servicer.py:1937-1944 — the gateway-supplied executor: `async def _execute(child_event): if child_event.tool in _SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS: return ...{\"error\": \"subagent_no_recursive_spawn\"}; return await self._dispatch_builtin(child_event, start, provider, file_state)`. It passes the PARENT's `start`. Inside _dispatch_builtin the only authorization is the parent permission gate (lines 1988-1993): `decision, rule_idx = self._permission_gate.resolve(event.tool, perm_ctx)` where perm_ctx is keyed on parent model/session_key/user_id — it has no knowledge of the child's narrowed allowlist. So a child whose schema hides run_shell/write_file, but whose model emits run_shell anyway, runs it with the parent's full authority.

(6) The docstring at agent_servicer.py:1925-1927 asserts 'it can never exceed the parent ... the child's tool *schemas* are already filtered' — conflating schema advertisement with enforcement.

(7) No test enforces this: tests/test_subagent_tool_execution.py covers execution/synthesis/budget but has zero allowlist-at-execution assertion; grep for child_tool_names / tool_not_in_allowlist across all tests returns nothing. The escalation gate (runner.py:710-815, _ToolAllowlistEscalationError) only rejects a caller REQUESTING tools outside the parent set at spawn time; it does not constrain which tools the child MODEL may emit at runtime.
refined_fix:
Enforce the resolved allowlist at the execution boundary so advertisement and execution agree. Minimal, runner-local fix:

1. runner.py:351-361 — pass the resolved set into the driver: add `allowed_tools=child_tool_names` to the `_drive_and_collect(...)` call.
2. runner.py:372-383 — add param `allowed_tools: frozenset[str] | None = None` to `_drive_and_collect` and forward it to `_drain_events` in the `_drain_events(...)` call at 412-423.
3. runner.py:586-596 — add `allowed_tools: frozenset[str] | None = None` to `_drain_events`.
4. runner.py:624-651 — in the ToolCallEvent branch, BEFORE `executed += 1` / `_execute_child_tool`, insert: `if allowed_tools is not None and event.tool not in allowed_tools: loop.feed_tool_result(ToolResult(call_id=event.call_id, content=json.dumps({\"error\": \"tool_not_in_allowlist\", \"tool\": event.tool}), is_error=True)); continue`. This keeps the conversation alive (model sees a typed envelope and adapts) and never executes an off-allowlist tool.

This requires no signature change to ChildToolExecutor or the gateway — the runner already holds `child_tool_names` and is the correct chokepoint. (Optionally also tighten agent_servicer.py:1937-1944 by threading the set into _make_child_tool_executor for defense-in-depth, but the runner gate alone closes the hole for all three spawn paths.) Add a test in tests/test_subagent_tool_execution.py: an executor that would run run_shell, a child allowlist of {web_search}, assert the run_shell call returns the tool_not_in_allowlist envelope and the executor is never invoked for it.

original.title: Child tool-allowlist is advisory only: the child tool executor runs ANY tool the parent holds, bypassing the runner's filtered subset (privilege non-containment)
original.evidence: The runner only filters the ADVERTISED schemas: `child_tools = _project_tool_schemas(parent_tools, child_tool_names)` then `ChatStart(..., tools=child_tools)`. But actual execution goes through `_make_child_tool_executor`, whose closure is `async def _execute(child_event): if child_event.tool in _SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS: return ...no_recursive_spawn; return await self._dispatch_builtin(child_event, start, provider, file_state)`. It passes the PARENT's `start` and only blocks the three spawn tools. There is no check that `child_event.tool` is in the child's resolved `tool_allowlist` / `card_tools_allowed` intersection. `_dispatch_builtin` applies only the parent's `_permission_gate`. So if the parent advertises {web_search, run_shell, write_file} and the spawn passes `tool_allowlist=["web_search"]` (or the card narrows to web_search), the child's *schema* hides run_shell/write_file, but if the child model emits a `run_shell` call anyway, the executor runs it with the parent's full authority. The docstring even claims 'it can never exceed the parent ... the child's tool schemas are already filtered' — filtering schemas is not enforcement. This violates Claude-Code parity rule #5 (children get a controlled subset, must not escalate): the per-spawn allowlist and card narrowing are silently unenforced at the execution boundary.
original.claude_code_parity: Claude Code enforces the subagent's tool subset at the tool-execution gate, not just by hiding schemas. Here the subset is purely cosmetic for any tool the parent itself holds, so a confused/adversarial child prompt can reach the parent's entire toolset (e.g. run_shell, write_file, apply_patch).
original.proposed_fix: Thread the child's resolved tool-name set (the `frozenset` returned by `_filter_tools_for_child`, or at minimum `spec.tool_allowlist` + card narrowing) into `_make_child_tool_executor`, and in `_execute` reject any `child_event.tool` not in that set with a typed `{"error": "tool_not_in_allowlist"}` envelope before calling `_dispatch_builtin`. The runner already computes this set; pass it down (e.g. via `run_child` -> a per-child executor factory) so advertisement and execution agree.

title: run_in_background is advertised only on subagent_spawn but unreachable end-to-end: gateway routing never threads the dispatcher, and the published dispatcher's factory is a hard RuntimeError with no replace_factory hook to fix it
proof:
CONFIRMED in current source.

(1) Routing never threads the dispatcher. agent_servicer.py calls dispatch_subagent_spawn (2045), dispatch_subagent_spawn_many (2080), dispatch_subagent_spawn_inline (2108) and NONE pass subagent_dispatcher=. `grep -n "subagent_dispatcher" agent_servicer.py` returns ZERO hits; there is no _get_subagent_dispatcher method. The param defaults to None (tool_wrapper.py:300: `subagent_dispatcher: Any | None = None`).

(2) None => guaranteed reject. tool_wrapper.py:392-405: `if parsed.run_in_background: if subagent_dispatcher is None: ... return _result_json(_rejected_result(... error=BACKGROUND_NOT_IMPLEMENTED_ERROR))`. Since routing never passes one, this branch always fires for subagent_spawn.

(3) The dispatcher that IS published has a hard-raise factory. entrypoint.py:1874-1885: `async def _unwired_run_child_factory(req): ... raise RuntimeError("subagent run_child factory not wired; W1.1 must install one via AdminState.subagent_dispatcher.replace_factory()")`, installed at 1896-1898 `AsyncSubagentDispatcher(store=..., run_child_factory=_unwired_run_child_factory, ...)` and published to admin_b_state.subagent_dispatcher (1904) + app.state (1906).

(4) No replace_factory exists. `grep "def replace_factory"` across python/ and rust/ returns nothing; the only occurrence of the name is the error string at entrypoint.py:1884. So the documented "wiring hook" is fictional.

(5) Even if the dispatcher were threaded, _run flips the row to failed. dispatcher.py:387-411: `result = await self._run_child_factory(req)` ... `except Exception as exc: ... await self._store.update(request_id, state="failed", ..., error=str(exc), finish_reason="error")`. Factory RuntimeError => row "failed".

(6) Schema inconsistency confirmed. Only subagent_spawn_tool_schema exposes run_in_background (tool_wrapper.py:188-200, description: "NOT YET IMPLEMENTED ... background dispatch lands in W1.3"). subagent_spawn_inline_tool_schema properties (1420-1486) and subagent_spawn_many_tool_schema per_task properties (1130-1177) have NO run_in_background field. Yet _parse_inline_args reuses _parse_args (1519) which parses run_in_background (853-856, 897) and re-exports it (1532: run_in_background=base.run_in_background); dispatch_subagent_spawn_inline then rejects with BACKGROUND_NOT_IMPLEMENTED_ERROR (1583-1590) for a field its schema never advertised. spawn_many's per_task schema neither advertises nor parses any per-task background flag, so it is silently ignored.

Net: the foreground path is correct and the R4-F3 "clean run_in_background_not_implemented" claim holds — but only because the dispatcher is never threaded. The codebase advertises a real-looking background surface on one of three sibling tools, publishes a dispatcher to AdminState, and names a replace_factory() install hook that does not exist; the executable background path is a guaranteed reject (None) or, if threaded, a guaranteed RuntimeError->failed. Dishonest/inconsistent grammar + dead feature. Not a crash or data-loss bug (foreground unaffected, rejects are clean), hence medium.
refined_fix:
Pick honesty-first (a) now, finish-wiring (b) later.

(a) Make the grammar honest immediately (smallest change): remove the run_in_background property from subagent_spawn_tool_schema (tool_wrapper.py:188-200) so no tool advertises a feature all three reject. Keep the defensive reject branches (tool_wrapper.py:392-405 and 1583-1590) since callers could still inject the key. Optionally have entrypoint.py NOT publish a dispatcher whose only factory hard-raises: either skip the AsyncSubagentDispatcher construction (entrypoint.py:1861-1908) until a real factory exists, or keep publishing it but make the routes' 503 the single source of truth rather than advertising W1.3 in the schema. This removes the false promise with ~3 line deletions.

(b) If background is actually wanted: (i) add AsyncSubagentDispatcher.replace_factory(factory) to system/subagent/dispatcher.py (the class already uses __slots__ with "_run_child_factory" at line 164, so just reassign self._run_child_factory); (ii) at gateway boot, build a real supervisor+registry+provider-bound run_child_factory and either pass it at construction (entrypoint.py:1896) or call replace_factory once the agent_servicer/registry is available; (iii) thread the published dispatcher into routing — add a servicer accessor returning app.state.corlinman_subagent_dispatcher and pass subagent_dispatcher=<that> into all three dispatch_subagent_spawn* calls (agent_servicer.py:2045, 2080, 2108); (iv) for parity, either add run_in_background to the inline + spawn_many (per_task) schemas and implement them, or assert in _parse_inline_args/_parse_args that the field is absent for those tools. Either way, the schema across all three tools must agree with what the executable path can deliver.

original.title: run_in_background is advertised+wired only on subagent_spawn; the live gateway path never passes a dispatcher so it always rejects, and the inline/many schemas omit the field entirely (inconsistent grammar + dead feature)
original.evidence: `subagent_spawn_tool_schema` advertises `run_in_background` (tool_wrapper.py:188) and `dispatch_subagent_spawn` has a full `_dispatch_via_background` path (392-412). But in agent_servicer routing, none of the three `dispatch_subagent_spawn*` calls (lines 2045, 2080, 2108) pass `subagent_dispatcher=`. The arg defaults to `None`, so `if subagent_dispatcher is None:` always fires -> `BACKGROUND_NOT_IMPLEMENTED_ERROR`. Worse, the dispatcher that IS constructed in entrypoint.py:1896 is wired with `_unwired_run_child_factory` which unconditionally `raise RuntimeError('subagent run_child factory not wired; W1.1 must install one via AdminState.subagent_dispatcher.replace_factory()')`. No `replace_factory` method exists anywhere (grep returns only the docstring mention), and nothing ever installs a real factory. So even if `subagent_dispatcher` were threaded, every background spawn would `_run` -> factory raises -> row flips to `failed`. Meanwhile `subagent_spawn_many` and `subagent_spawn_inline` schemas do NOT expose `run_in_background` at all, yet `_parse_inline_args` still parses it and rejects with `BACKGROUND_NOT_IMPLEMENTED_ERROR` (1583), and spawn_many silently ignores any per-task background flag. The R4-F3 claim ('clean run_in_background_not_implemented') holds for the *foreground* path only by accident (dispatcher never threaded), but the codebase promises a background surface it cannot deliver: the schema tells the model the feature is real and 'lands in W1.3', the dispatcher exists and is published to AdminState, but the executable path is a guaranteed RuntimeError.
original.claude_code_parity: Claude Code's Task tool background/async dispatch actually returns a request_id and later notifies. Here the entire async surface is non-functional (placeholder factory that always raises) while advertised as a real-but-unimplemented field, and the field is inconsistently present across the three sibling tools.
original.proposed_fix: Either (a) remove `run_in_background` from the spawn schema until a real factory is wired, or (b) finish the wiring: implement `AsyncSubagentDispatcher.replace_factory()`, install a real supervisor-bound `run_child_factory` at gateway boot, and thread `subagent_dispatcher=self._get_subagent_dispatcher()` into all three routing calls. At minimum, make the schema honest across all three tools (either all expose it or none) and have entrypoint not publish a dispatcher whose factory is a hard raise.

title: _get_subagent_caps (and runner.py / tool_wrapper.py) docstrings cite stale "3 per-parent" cap after raise to 10
proof: agent_servicer.py:2614-2615 docstring of `_get_subagent_caps`: "Lazy-constructs the shared in-process :class:`Supervisor` (default\n        policy: 3 per-parent, 15 per-tenant, depth 2)". But the constructed policy is the default `SupervisorPolicy()` (agent_servicer.py:2629 `sup = Supervisor(SupervisorPolicy())`), whose default is supervisor.py:102 `max_concurrent_per_parent: int = 10` (supervisor.py:91 docstring: "max_concurrent_per_parent=10 (v1.12.2 — raised 3 -> 10 ...)"). The same stale "3" is repeated at runner.py:95 ("supervisor's per-parent concurrency cap (default 3)") and tool_wrapper.py:1242 ("per-parent concurrency cap (default 3)"). This is also internally inconsistent: tool_wrapper.py:1096-1104 already document the lock-step bump and set `SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_MAX_TASKS: int = 10`. With both spawn_many max-tasks (10) and per-parent cap (10) now equal, the "3" docstrings misdescribe the live fan-out limit by >3x and obscure that spawn_many can exactly saturate the per-parent cap with zero headroom. Per-tenant (15) and depth (2) values in the docstring are correct.
refined_fix: Three doc edits, no logic change. (1) agent_servicer.py:2615 change "3 per-parent" -> "10 per-parent". (2) runner.py:95 change "(default 3)" -> "(default 10)". (3) tool_wrapper.py:1242 change "(default 3)" -> "(default 10)". Better/robust: have these strings interpolate `SupervisorPolicy().max_concurrent_per_parent` (and `.max_concurrent_per_tenant` / `.max_depth`) rather than hardcoding, so the next raise can't desync them — e.g. build the agent_servicer docstring/comment from the dataclass defaults, and reference `SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_MAX_TASKS` / the policy field in the runner.py and tool_wrapper.py comments.

original.title: _get_subagent_caps docstring states the wrong policy values (3 per-parent) after the cap was raised to 10, misleading operators reading the entry point
original.evidence: `_get_subagent_caps` docstring: 'Lazy-constructs the shared in-process :class:`Supervisor` (default policy: 3 per-parent, 15 per-tenant, depth 2)'. But the actual `SupervisorPolicy()` it constructs (supervisor.py:102) is `max_concurrent_per_parent: int = 10`. The '3 per-parent' value is stale (it was raised 3->10 in v1.12.2 per the SupervisorPolicy docstring). The runner.py module constants (lines 97, comment line) and `dispatch_subagent_spawn_many` docstring (tool_wrapper.py:1243 'per-parent concurrency cap (default 3)') repeat the same stale '3'. Not a runtime bug, but every comment describing the live fan-out limit is off by >3x, which will mislead the next person reasoning about whether spawn_many (cap 10) can ever exceed the per-parent cap (now also 10 -> exactly saturates, no headroom).
original.claude_code_parity: N/A (documentation drift).
original.proposed_fix: Update the docstrings in `_get_subagent_caps` (agent_servicer.py:2615), `dispatch_subagent_spawn_many` (tool_wrapper.py:1243), and runner.py:97 to say '10 per-parent' to match the live `SupervisorPolicy` default. Better: have these strings interpolate `SupervisorPolicy().max_concurrent_per_parent` instead of hardcoding.

title: spawn_many per-task schema requires `agent`, but dispatch only requires `goal` and defaults a missing agent to general-purpose (schema stricter than behavior; disagrees with single-spawn)
proof:
CONFIRMED in current source.

(1) spawn_many per-task schema, line 1179: `"required": ["agent", "goal"],` with `"additionalProperties": False,` (line 1180).

(2) single-spawn schema, line 259: `"required": ["goal"],` — `subagent_type`/`agent` are advertised as Optional (lines 166-176, 212-219). So the two sibling tools disagree on whether the agent field is mandatory.

(3) Fan-out wrapper does NOT enforce `required:["agent"]`. `_parse_spawn_many_args` (1373-1382) only checks each task is a dict then re-serialises verbatim: `out.append(json.dumps(task))`. It delegates per-sibling validation to `_parse_args` via `dispatch_subagent_spawn` (1281-1300).

(4) `_parse_args` hard-requires ONLY goal — line 821-823: `goal = raw.get("goal"); if not isinstance(goal, str) or not goal: raise _ArgsInvalidError("missing or empty 'goal' field")`. The agent field is explicitly optional — docstring line 800-801: "`goal` is the only hard requirement — every other field is optional"; lines 829-846 resolve `subagent_type = None` when both `subagent_type` and `agent` are absent.

(5) A missing agent silently runs general-purpose — line 423: `card = agent_registry.get_or_builtin_default(parsed.subagent_type)` called with `None`, and lines 416-422 document this returns "an IN-CODE `general-purpose` fallback when the field is empty/absent". Only an *explicit-but-unknown* value rejects (lines 424-434).

Net: a lax (non-strict) OpenAI-compatible provider lets an agent-less fan-out task through and silently runs general-purpose; a strict provider 400s the whole spawn_many call on the missing `required` field. Provider-dependent inconsistency is real.

Secondary claim (per-task schema omits subagent_type/model/run_in_background/description) also CONFIRMED: per_task.properties (1130-1177) lists only agent/goal/tool_allowlist/max_wall_seconds/max_tool_calls/extra_context, and `additionalProperties:False` (1180) means a strict provider REJECTS those single-spawn fields if a model emits them; a lax provider passes them through json.dumps(task) into _parse_args and honors them.

CORRECTION to the finding's wording: it claims `description` "is never threaded by _parse_spawn_many_args -> the per-sibling spawns lose the activity-panel label." Imprecise — _parse_spawn_many_args re-serialises the whole task verbatim, so an emitted `description` DOES survive into _parse_args (848-851). The real reason fan-out children get no activity-panel label is that the *synchronous* spawn path never emits description at all: _run_child_under_slot (482-561) and _emit_subagent_spawned (652-683) carry only prompt_preview=spec.goal, no description; description is only threaded on the *background* path (line 1007, SubagentRequest.description). So that label gap exists for single-spawn synchronous calls equally, not as a fan-out-specific regression.
refined_fix:
tool_wrapper.py:1179 — change the per-task schema's required from `["agent", "goal"]` to `["goal"]` so the advertised contract matches the actual _parse_args behavior (agent optional, defaults to general-purpose). This is the minimal correctness fix and aligns the two sibling tools.

Optionally, for parity with single-spawn, extend the per_task.properties dict (1130-1177) to also advertise `subagent_type`, `model`, and `description` (copying the descriptions from lines 166-211), keeping `additionalProperties:False` so a model trained on single-spawn fields isn't silently rejected by strict providers or dropped. Do NOT add `run_in_background` to per_task — per-sibling background dispatch inside a gather is not a supported pattern; if added, _parse_spawn_many_args should strip/reject it.

If instead the intent is that fan-out only supports the named-agent subset, keep `required:["agent"]` but then make _parse_spawn_many_args (1373-1382) enforce that each task carries a non-empty `agent`/`subagent_type` so behavior matches the schema on BOTH lax and strict providers — and document the subset in the tool description (1186-1195).

original.title: subagent_spawn_many per-task schema marks 'agent' as required, but the validation/dispatch treats a missing agent as the general-purpose default — schema is stricter than behavior
original.evidence: The per-task schema for spawn_many is `"required": ["agent", "goal"]` (line 1179). But `_parse_spawn_many_args` only validates each task is a dict and re-serialises it; the actual per-sibling validation is `_parse_args`, whose only hard requirement is `goal` (line 822-823) — a missing `agent`/`subagent_type` falls through to `agent_registry.get_or_builtin_default(None)` -> general-purpose card. So a provider that does not strictly enforce JSON-Schema `required` (many OpenAI-compatible backends are lax) lets an agent-less task through and silently runs general-purpose, while a strict provider 400s the whole call. The single-spawn schema correctly makes the agent field optional (`required: ['goal']`), so the two sibling tools disagree on whether `agent` is mandatory. Also note spawn_many's per-task schema omits `subagent_type`, `model`, `run_in_background`, and `description` that the single path accepts, so a model trained on single-spawn fields will have those silently dropped (description in particular is never threaded by _parse_spawn_many_args -> the per-sibling spawns lose the activity-panel label).
original.claude_code_parity: Minor: Claude Code keeps the per-task spec shape consistent with the single-task spawn. Here the fan-out task spec is a divergent, narrower shape with an inconsistent required-set.
original.proposed_fix: Make the per-task schema's `required` match actual behavior (`["goal"]`, treating `agent` as optional with general-purpose default), and align the per-task properties with the single-spawn schema (add `subagent_type`, `model`, `description`) so fan-out children get the same fields — or explicitly document that fan-out only supports the named-agent subset.

title: Runner advertises spawn tools to mid-depth children (max_depth>=3) that the depth-blind child executor unconditionally refuses
proof:
The two gates are confirmed in current source and DO diverge for max_depth>=3.

RUNNER GATE (advertise side) — runner.py:822-825, in _filter_tools_for_child:
  `if child_depth >= max_depth - 1:`
  `    effective.discard(SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOL)`
  `    effective.discard(SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_TOOL)`
  `    effective.discard(SUBAGENT_SPAWN_INLINE_TOOL)`
This is the ONLY place spawn tools are stripped. The result feeds child_tools (runner.py:295 `_project_tool_schemas`) which becomes the child's advertised ChatStart.tools (runner.py:338 `tools=child_tools`).

EXECUTOR GATE (execute side) — agent_servicer.py:1937-1942, _make_child_tool_executor:
  `async def _execute(child_event: ToolCallEvent) -> str:`
  `    if child_event.tool in _SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS:`
  `        return json.dumps({"error": "subagent_no_recursive_spawn"})`
  `    return await self._dispatch_builtin(child_event, start, provider, file_state)`
This is depth-blind: it is built ONCE from the parent's depth-0 `start` (agent_servicer.py:2062/2091/2120 `tool_executor=self._make_child_tool_executor(start, provider, file_state)`) and refuses ALL three spawn tools (_SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS, agent_servicer.py:254-256) regardless of the child's depth. The runner threads this same callback straight into the child loop (runner.py:358 -> 419 -> _execute_child_tool at 652) and never builds a depth-aware alternative.

DEPTH ARITHMETIC: parent_ctx is depth=0 (agent_servicer.py:1972 `depth=0`); child_context increments (types.py:313 `depth=min(self.depth + 1, 255)`), so the first spawned child is depth=1.
- max_depth=2 (production default; SupervisorPolicy.max_depth=2 at supervisor.py:104, confirmed at _get_subagent_caps docstring 2615): depth-1 child -> `1 >= 2-1=1` -> True -> spawn tools PRUNED. Gates AGREE. No bug at default.
- max_depth=3 (operator-configurable; SupervisorPolicy is a plain mutable dataclass, max_depth:int=2): depth-1 child -> `1 >= 3-1=2` -> False -> spawn tools LEFT in the child's advertised ChatStart.tools, yet the depth-blind executor still returns {"error":"subagent_no_recursive_spawn"} on every such call. Advertised set != usable set; child can burn reasoning rounds calling un-runnable tools.

No alternate depth-aware executor or supervisor re-dispatch path exists that would refute this. The finding's quotes, line numbers, and reasoning all match current source.
refined_fix:
Option (a) from the finding is the minimal, correct fix and keeps advertised==usable for ALL max_depth values, matching the executor's unconditional refusal. In runner.py:822 change the self-prune gate from the max_depth-relative condition to an absolute "any spawned child" condition:

  python/packages/corlinman-agent/src/corlinman_agent/subagent/runner.py:822
  - if child_depth >= max_depth - 1:
  + if child_depth >= 1:   # every spawned child; mirrors the executor's blanket recursive-spawn refusal

Since the first spawned child is always depth>=1, this strips spawn tools from every child for any max_depth, exactly matching _make_child_tool_executor's blanket refusal. Update the now-stale docstrings/comments referencing the "max_depth - 1" / "deepest legal depth" rule (runner.py:18, 82, 98, 106, 205-207, 776, 817-825; tool_wrapper.py:344) to state the depth>=1 rule. NOTE: this is purely a correctness/consistency cleanup for the non-default max_depth>=3 configuration; at the shipped default max_depth=2 there is no behavioral change and no user-visible bug today. Option (b) (a depth-aware executor that actually re-dispatches nested spawns under the child's incremented ParentContext through the supervisor's depth gate) is the larger change and only worth it if real grandchild spawning is a desired feature — not required to close this inconsistency.

original.title: Depth self-prune fires at max_depth-1 but the executor unconditionally refuses ALL recursive spawns, so for max_depth>=3 children are advertised spawn tools they can never execute
original.evidence: The runner only strips the spawn tools when `child_depth >= max_depth - 1` (runner.py:822 `if child_depth >= max_depth - 1: effective.discard(SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOL); ...discard(SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_TOOL); ...discard(SUBAGENT_SPAWN_INLINE_TOOL)`). With the default `DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH=2` this correctly strips spawn tools from every depth-1 child, satisfying Claude-Code semantic #3. BUT the production child tool executor refuses ALL recursive spawns unconditionally, regardless of depth: `_make_child_tool_executor` returns `async def _execute(child_event): if child_event.tool in _SUBAGENT_SPAWN_TOOLS: return json.dumps({"error": "subagent_no_recursive_spawn"})` (agent_servicer.py:1937-1939). So if an operator raises `SupervisorPolicy.max_depth` to 3+, a depth-1 child gets `1 >= 3-1=2` => False => the runner LEAVES subagent_spawn/spawn_many/spawn_inline in the child's advertised `ChatStart.tools`, yet every such call the child emits is hard-rejected with `subagent_no_recursive_spawn`. The child wastes reasoning rounds calling tools that can never run, and the advertised toolset diverges from the actual capability. This is the SAFE direction (no escalation, no past-ceiling spawn), but it is an advertised-vs-real-capability inconsistency.
original.claude_code_parity: Claude Code strips the Task tool from the advertised set at the depth ceiling so the model never sees a tool it cannot use. Here the strip and the hard refusal use two different depth predicates (runner: depth>=max_depth-1; executor: always-on), so they only agree when max_depth==2. For max_depth>=3 the child is advertised spawn tools it can never execute, diverging from the 'advertised toolset == usable toolset' contract.
original.proposed_fix: Make the two gates agree. Either (a) have the runner strip spawn tools whenever the child is at any depth>=1 (i.e. for every spawned child, matching the executor's unconditional refusal) by pruning at `child_depth >= 1` rather than `child_depth >= max_depth - 1`; or (b) make `_make_child_tool_executor` depth-aware and actually re-dispatch a nested spawn under the child's real (incremented) ParentContext when depth still permits, so the supervisor's depth gate -- not a blanket refusal -- is the single source of truth. Option (a) is the smaller, safer change and keeps the advertised set == usable set for all max_depth values.

title: _get_subagent_caps docstring (and two sibling comments) state stale '3 per-parent' cap; live SupervisorPolicy default is 10 since v1.12.2
proof:
agent_servicer.py:2614-2615 docstring: "Lazy-constructs the shared in-process :class:`Supervisor` (default
        policy: 3 per-parent, 15 per-tenant, depth 2)". The constructed default is `Supervisor(SupervisorPolicy())` (agent_servicer.py:2629). The live SupervisorPolicy default is `max_concurrent_per_parent: int = 10` (supervisor.py:102), with the in-code note "max_concurrent_per_parent=10 (v1.12.2 — raised 3 -> 10 to match Claude Code's Task-tool max-fanout and SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_MAX_TASKS)" (supervisor.py:91-94). The same stale '3' recurs at tool_wrapper.py:1242 ("The supervisor's
    per-parent concurrency cap (default 3) is the hard limit on live
    siblings") and runner.py:95 ("The
#: supervisor's per-parent concurrency cap (default 3) still bounds the
#: live siblings"). Cross-ref SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_MAX_TASKS: int = 10 (tool_wrapper.py:1104). No behavioral impact: enforcement reads the live value `if cur_parent >= self._policy.max_concurrent_per_parent` (supervisor.py:340), never the literal 3, so the comments are misleading-only. The '15 per-tenant' and 'depth 2' parts of the docstring remain accurate (max_concurrent_per_tenant=15 supervisor.py:103, max_depth=2 supervisor.py:104).
refined_fix: Pure docstring/comment fix, no code change. (1) agent_servicer.py:2615 — change "3 per-parent" to "10 per-parent" (leave "15 per-tenant, depth 2" as-is, both correct). (2) tool_wrapper.py:1242 — change "(default 3)" to "(default 10)". (3) runner.py:95 — change "(default 3)" to "(default 10)". Note: the finding cited runner.py:97 for this comment, but the "(default 3)" text is actually on line 95 (the SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_TOOL constant itself is at line 99). To prevent future drift, prefer phrasing the cap as "per-parent cap = SupervisorPolicy.max_concurrent_per_parent (default 10)" so the doc references the constant rather than re-stating the literal.

original.title: _get_subagent_caps docstring claims '3 per-parent' but SupervisorPolicy default is now 10 (stale after the v1.12.2 fanout bump)
original.evidence: `_get_subagent_caps` constructs `Supervisor(SupervisorPolicy())` (agent_servicer.py:2629) but its docstring states 'Lazy-constructs the shared in-process :class:`Supervisor` (default policy: 3 per-parent, 15 per-tenant, depth 2)'. The actual default is `max_concurrent_per_parent: int = 10` (supervisor.py:102), raised 3->10 in v1.12.2 in lock-step with `SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_MAX_TASKS=10`. The same stale '3' appears in the dispatcher docstring for `dispatch_subagent_spawn_many` ('per-parent concurrency cap (default 3)', tool_wrapper.py:1243) and in the SUBAGENT_SPAWN_MANY_TOOL constant comment (runner.py:97 'per-parent concurrency cap (default 3)'). title: Child drain task leaks on parent cancellation (asyncio.shield with no finally-cancel)
proof: runner.py:412 `drain_task: asyncio.Task[None] = asyncio.ensure_future(_drain_events(...))`. The try block at 424 has ONLY `except TimeoutError:` (433) and `except Exception as exc:` (455) and then falls through at 463 to `output_text = "".join(output_chunks)` (464) and `return TaskResult(...)` (496-504) — there is NO `finally`. The sole await is `await asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.shield(drain_task), timeout=float(task.max_wall_seconds))` (429-432). `drain_task.cancel()` appears ONLY in the timeout-grace-exhausted branch (448), reachable only after `except TimeoutError` fires. On a PARENT cancel (not a timeout) the CancelledError exits `wait_for` but `asyncio.shield` leaves `drain_task` running. I reproduced the exact semantics: a coroutine awaiting `asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.shield(drain_task), ...)` that is then cancelled by its parent leaves the inner worker `done=False, cancelled=False`, and the worker ran to full completion after the parent was already cancelled (`drain_ran_to_completion = True`, `LIVE TASKS still running after parent cancel: 1`). The cancellation path is live and unshielded end-to-end: tool_wrapper.py:1301 `raw = await asyncio.gather(*coros, return_exceptions=True)` over plain `dispatch_subagent_spawn(...)` coros (1281-1300) -> `_run_child_under_slot` -> `result = await run_child(...)` (572) inside `with slot_cm:` (a sync CM that does not absorb cancel) -> runner.py:351 `return await _drive_and_collect(...)`. A parent-turn or gather cancel therefore reaches the shielded await and orphans the inner `_drain_events` task, which keeps holding the live ReasoningLoop, provider stream, and tool_executor.
refined_fix: In `_drive_and_collect` (runner.py:424-462) add a `finally:` clause to the existing `try` (no new imports needed — `asyncio` is imported at line 41 and `contextlib` at line 42). After the `except Exception` block ending at line 462, append: `finally:` / `if not drain_task.done():` / `drain_task.cancel()` / `with contextlib.suppress(asyncio.CancelledError, Exception):` / `await drain_task`. This force-cancels the shielded worker on every exit path — including parent/gather CancelledError — releasing the ReasoningLoop/provider-stream/tool_executor. The `await drain_task` is safe and bounded because the task is already-done in the normal path and is being cancelled otherwise. Note: awaiting a cancelled task inside a `finally` while the surrounding frame is itself being cancelled is fine here because the suppress swallows the re-raised CancelledError; keep the outer CancelledError propagating by NOT swallowing it at the call boundary (the suppress only guards the `await drain_task`). The timeout case is unaffected: the TimeoutError branch already drains cooperatively and explicitly cancels at 448, so by the time `finally` runs `drain_task.done()` is typically True and the cancel is a no-op.

original.title: Child drain task leaks on parent cancellation (asyncio.shield without finally-cancel)
original.evidence: `_drive_and_collect` creates the inner worker as `drain_task = asyncio.ensure_future(_drain_events(...))` (line 412) and then awaits it only through `await asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.shield(drain_task), timeout=float(task.max_wall_seconds))` (lines 429-432). `drain_task` is cancelled ONLY inside the timeout-grace-exhausted branch (`drain_task.cancel()`, line 448). There is no `try/finally` around the whole function that cancels `drain_task`. Because of `asyncio.shield`, when the PARENT cancels `run_child`/`_drive_and_collect` (parent turn cancelled, or the `dispatch_subagent_spawn_many` gather is cancelled), the `CancelledError` propagates out of `wait_for` but the shielded `drain_task` is NOT cancelled — it keeps running detached, holding the live `ReasoningLoop`, the provider stream, and the `tool_executor`. This is a genuine task/connection leak on every parent-cancellation path (the spawn_many fan-out cancels its coros on cancellation, which is exactly the path that hits this).
original.proposed_fix: Wrap the body in `try/finally` and, in the `finally`, `if not drain_task.done(): drain_task.cancel()` followed by `with contextlib.suppress(asyncio.CancelledError, Exception): await drain_task`. Equivalently, drop the outer `asyncio.shield` so a parent cancel propagates into the worker — the shield is only needed to keep the partial output after a *timeout*, which is already handled separately by the explicit grace-period cancel in the TimeoutError branch.
original.claude_code_parity: N/A (resource-leak hazard, not a parity rule)

title: Supervisor slot counters leak on cancellation in the acquire→`with slot_cm` window in `_run_child_under_slot`
proof:
CONFIRMED REAL in current code. In `_run_child_under_slot` (tool_wrapper.py):

L532: `outcome = supervisor_acquire(parent_ctx)`
L550: `slot_cm = outcome  # context-manager-shaped slot drop-guard`
L553: `child_ctx_preview = parent_ctx.child_context(agent_name, child_seq)`  (sync)
L554-561: `await _emit_subagent_spawned(emitter=event_emitter, ...)`  ← SUSPENSION POINT, OUTSIDE the guard
L570: `try:`
L571: `    with slot_cm:`  ← guard entered only here

The production binding makes the leak concrete. agent_servicer.py `_get_subagent_caps` (L2634-2640) binds `_acquire` to `sup.try_acquire(parent_ctx)`, and `Supervisor.try_acquire` (supervisor.py L339-351) SYNCHRONOUSLY increments both counters before returning the Slot:
  L343: `self._per_parent[parent_key] = cur_parent + 1`
  L351: `self._per_tenant[tenant_key] = cur_tenant + 1`
So by L550 the counters are already incremented; only `Slot.release()` (supervisor.py L169-175) decrements them, and release is reachable only via the `with slot_cm:` __exit__ (L571), an explicit release, or `__del__` (L183-191).

`_emit_subagent_spawned` (tool_wrapper.py L652-689) genuinely awaits: L673 `await emitter.emit_event(...)`. Its `except Exception` (L684) does NOT catch `asyncio.CancelledError` (a BaseException subclass in py3.8+), so a cancel at this await propagates straight out of `_run_child_under_slot` BEFORE L571 — the `with slot_cm:` block is never entered, `Slot.release()` is never called, and `_per_parent`/`_per_tenant` stay incremented. The caller at L459 is a bare `return await _run_child_under_slot(...)` with no try/finally backstop. The only reclaim path is `Slot.__del__` (supervisor.py L183-191), whose asyncio GC timing is non-deterministic.

Contrast confirms intent: the sibling in-process path `Supervisor.spawn_child` (supervisor.py L699-721) wraps its only await (`asyncio.wait_for`, L703) INSIDE `with slot:` (L700) and uses a synchronous `emit_spawned` (L689) — no leak. The tool_wrapper path diverges by awaiting a separate emit outside the guard. test_subagent_runner.py L381-418 already asserts "the per-parent counter ... released afterwards" for the timeout path, but no test covers cancellation in this acquire→guard window.
refined_fix:
Move the slot guard up so every suspension point after acquisition runs inside it. In tool_wrapper.py `_run_child_under_slot`, replace the structure at L552-585 so `with slot_cm:` opens immediately after `slot_cm = outcome` (i.e. wrap from L553 `child_ctx_preview = ...` onward, including the `await _emit_subagent_spawned(...)` at L554, the `_make_bubble_emitter` call, and the existing `try/except` around `run_child`). Concretely:

    with slot_cm:
        child_ctx_preview = parent_ctx.child_context(agent_name, child_seq)
        await _emit_subagent_spawned(...)
        _child_emitter = _make_bubble_emitter(...)
        try:
            result = await run_child(...)
        except Exception as exc:
            ...build ERROR TaskResult...

This is safe for the test path too: when `supervisor_acquire is None`, `slot_cm = nullcontext()` (L530), which is a valid re-entrant context manager. A cancel at the emit await now unwinds through `Slot.__exit__` → `Slot.release()` (idempotent), decrementing both counters deterministically instead of relying on `Slot.__del__` GC timing. Add a regression test (e.g. in test_subagent_spawn_inline.py / test_subagent_runner.py) that wires an emitter whose `emit_event` blocks, cancels the dispatch task at that await, and asserts `supervisor.parent_count(...) == 0` and `supervisor.tenant_count(...) == 0` afterward.

original.title: Supervisor slot counters leak if parent is cancelled in the acquire→`with slot_cm` window
original.evidence: In `_run_child_under_slot`, the slot is acquired (counters already incremented inside `Supervisor.try_acquire`) at `outcome = supervisor_acquire(parent_ctx)` / `slot_cm = outcome` (lines 532, 550), but the drop-guard is only entered later at `with slot_cm:` (line 571). Between them is `await _emit_subagent_spawned(...)` (lines 554-561) — a suspension point. If the parent turn is cancelled while suspended at that await, `CancelledError` propagates and the `with slot_cm:` block is never entered, so `Slot.release()` is never called. The per-parent and per-tenant counters stay incremented. The only backstop is `Slot.__del__` (supervisor.py:183-191), whose timing under asyncio is non-deterministic. With `max_concurrent_per_tenant=15`, a few cancellations in this window permanently shrink the tenant's available concurrency until GC reclaims the orphaned Slot.
original.proposed_fix: Enter the slot guard immediately after acquisition: move the `with slot_cm:` (or an explicit `try:` whose `finally` calls `slot.release()`) to wrap everything from line 553 onward, so the `_emit_subagent_spawned` await happens INSIDE the guard. Then a cancel at the emit await releases the slot on unwind.

title: Supervisor._lock is dead code; module/__init__ docstrings falsely claim it guards the counter read-modify-write
proof: grep across the whole subagent/agent source shows `_lock` appears exactly twice: in `__slots__` (line 223 `"_lock",`) and its assignment (line 275 `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()`). It is NEVER acquired — there is no `async with self._lock`, no `.acquire()`, no `await` referencing it anywhere. Yet the module docstring (lines 34-38) states: "The supervisor uses :class:`asyncio.Lock` around the counter read-modify-write so two concurrent ``try_acquire`` calls on the same parent see consistent counts." And the __init__ comment (lines 271-274) states: "Single asyncio lock guards both counter dicts... one global lock is simpler and just as correct (the read-modify-write window doesn't span any ``await``)." Meanwhile `try_acquire` (lines 316-353) mutates `_per_parent`/`_per_tenant` (lines 339, 343, 348, 351) with no lock, and is fully synchronous — its own docstring (lines 325-328) even says "Synchronous (no ``await``)". The decrement helpers `_dec_parent` (298-305) and `_dec_tenant` (307-312) are likewise sync and lock-free. So the lock is genuinely dead code, and the two docstrings misrepresent the concurrency model: atomicity comes from the await-free read-modify-write on the single-threaded loop, not from the (never-taken) lock.
refined_fix: Minimal fix — remove the dead lock and correct the prose (matches the real sync/atomic behavior). In supervisor.py: (1) delete `"_lock",` from `__slots__` at line 223; (2) delete `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()` and its preceding comment (lines 271-275); (3) rewrite the module docstring at lines 34-38 to state that on a single-threaded asyncio loop the counter read-modify-write in `try_acquire` is atomic because it spans no `await`, rather than claiming an `asyncio.Lock` is used. Optionally drop the now-unused `import asyncio` only if no other asyncio reference remains (it is still used by `asyncio.Lock`-free code such as `asyncio.wait_for`/`asyncio.Task`/`asyncio.get_event_loop`, so keep the import). Alternative (heavier, not recommended): actually wrap the counter mutations in `async with self._lock` and make `try_acquire`/`_dec_*` async — but that propagates async through `Slot.release`/`__del__`/`__exit__` and is unjustified for the current sync model.

original.title: Supervisor._lock is created but never acquired; module/init docstrings claim it guards the counter read-modify-write

This is harmless in practice ONLY because `try_acquire` is fully synchronous (no `await` spans the read-modify-write), so on the single-threaded loop it is atomic — but the lock is dead code and the docstrings actively misrepresent the concurrency model, which is a maintenance trap (a future edit that adds an `await` inside `try_acquire` would silently introduce a race the docstring claims is already covered).
original.proposed_fix: Either remove `_lock` and the docstring claims, or actually take `async with self._lock` around the counter mutations and make `try_acquire` async. Removing is simpler and matches the real (sync, atomic) behavior; update the docstrings to say the atomicity comes from the synchronous read-modify-write on a single-threaded loop, not from the lock.

title: AsyncSubagentDispatcher has no shutdown/cancel-all for in-flight background _tasks
proof: CONFIRMED in current source. (1) The class registers every background task: line 205 `self._tasks: dict[str, DispatchOutcome] = {}`; lines 317-321 `task = asyncio.create_task(self._run(req), name=f"subagent.background:{req.request_id}")` then line 321 `self._tasks[req.request_id] = DispatchOutcome(req.request_id, task)`. (2) The ONLY cancellation method is single-request `kill()` (lines 331-342): `outcome = self._tasks.get(request_id)` / `if outcome is not None and not outcome.task.done(): outcome.task.cancel()` — it takes a `request_id` and never iterates `self._tasks`. grep for `def shutdown|def aclose|def __aexit__|def cancel_all|def drain|def stop` over the whole file returns nothing; the dispatcher has no lifecycle teardown method at all. (3) Lifespan teardown (entrypoint.py ~2380-2432) `aclose()`s mcp_manager/update_checker/clawhub_client but never references the dispatcher, so in-flight tasks are left to loop-teardown. (4) `_run` already folds cancellation into a typed exit: lines 388-394 `except asyncio.CancelledError: ... await self._cleanup_registry(request_id); raise` — so a cancel-all is safe, as the finding claims. Reachability claims also verified: agent_servicer.py:2045-2068 calls `dispatch_subagent_spawn(...)` WITHOUT `subagent_dispatcher=`, so the param defaults to None (tool_wrapper.py:300) and the background branch at tool_wrapper.py:393 always returns BACKGROUND_NOT_IMPLEMENTED_ERROR; thus `dispatch_async` (the only `create_task` caller) is never invoked from the tool route. The installed factory is the stub `_unwired_run_child_factory` (entrypoint.py:1874-1885) which only `raise RuntimeError(...)`; the `replace_factory()` it names does not exist anywhere (grep `def replace_factory` repo-wide = empty). So no real background child can run today — defect is latent, hence severity low is correct.
refined_fix: Add to AsyncSubagentDispatcher in dispatcher.py (after `kill`, ~line 369): `async def shutdown(self) -> None:` that snapshots outcomes under `self._lock` (`async with self._lock: outcomes = list(self._tasks.values())`), cancels each undone task outside the lock (`for o in outcomes: if not o.task.done(): o.task.cancel()`), then `await asyncio.gather(*(o.task for o in outcomes), return_exceptions=True)`. This is safe because `_run` already converts CancelledError into a clean exit (dispatcher.py:388-394) and `_cleanup_registry` pops the row. Then call it from the gateway lifespan teardown in entrypoint.py (alongside the existing `await mcp_manager.aclose()` block ~line 2380): `dispatcher = getattr(app.state, "corlinman_subagent_dispatcher", None); if dispatcher is not None: await dispatcher.shutdown()`. Given the path is dead until the factory is wired, this can ship together with the W1.1 factory wiring rather than urgently.

Note the extras route does NOT fix the import cycle (corlinman-server already depends on corlinman-subagent at corlinman-server/pyproject.toml:82), so the child_emitter deletion is still the right move for the corlinman_server import specifically.

title: Supervisor._lock is dead code; module/__init__ docstrings claim it guards the counter read-modify-write but it is never acquired
proof: In python/packages/corlinman-subagent/src/corlinman_subagent/supervisor.py, `grep -rn "_lock"` over src/ returns exactly two hits: line 223 `"_lock",` (the __slots__ declaration) and line 275 `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()` (creation). There is NO `async with self._lock` anywhere in the file (grep for "async with" in supervisor.py returns nothing). The _lock references in dispatcher.py/store.py belong to unrelated classes (SubagentDispatcher / store), not Supervisor, and those DO use their locks. Yet the module docstring (lines 34-38) asserts: "The supervisor uses :class:`asyncio.Lock` around the counter read-modify-write so two concurrent ``try_acquire`` calls on the same parent see consistent counts." and the __init__ comment (lines 271-274) repeats: "Single asyncio lock guards both counter dicts ... the read-modify-write window doesn't span any ``await``." The actual mutation path `try_acquire` is declared synchronous: line 316 `def try_acquire(self, parent_ctx: ParentContext) -> Slot:` (not `async def`), and its read-modify-write body at lines 339-343 (`cur_parent = self._per_parent.get(parent_key, 0)` ... `self._per_parent[parent_key] = cur_parent + 1`) and 346-351 contains zero `await` and never touches `self._lock`. So the code is currently safe ONLY because try_acquire is await-free (no coroutine can interleave between read and write on a single-threaded loop); the lock is dead code and the documented "uses asyncio.Lock around the read-modify-write" invariant is literally false. If an `await` is ever added inside try_acquire (e.g. an async hook emit before the increment), the unguarded read-modify-write on _per_parent/_per_tenant becomes a real lost-update race on the concurrency cap.
refined_fix: Prefer deleting the dead lock and correcting the docstrings, since asyncio.Lock can only be acquired from an async method and try_acquire is intentionally synchronous: (1) Remove `"_lock",` from __slots__ at supervisor.py:223 and `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()` at supervisor.py:275. (2) Rewrite the module docstring lines 34-38 and the __init__ comment lines 271-274 to state plainly that correctness relies on try_acquire being fully synchronous (no await between the per_parent/per_tenant read and write), not on any lock. (3) Add a short comment/assert at the top of try_acquire (supervisor.py ~330) warning that no `await` may be introduced into the acquire path or the cap counters lose their lost-update safety. Alternatively, if a lock is genuinely wanted, make try_acquire `async def` and wrap the read-modify-write in `async with self._lock:` and update all callers (spawn_child:683) to await it — but this is heavier and unnecessary given the current await-free design.

Yet the module docstring states 'The supervisor uses asyncio.Lock around the counter read-modify-write so two concurrent try_acquire calls on the same parent see consistent counts' (34-38) and the __init__ comment repeats it (271-274). `try_acquire`, `_dec_parent`, `_dec_tenant` mutate `_per_parent`/`_per_tenant` with no lock. On a single-threaded asyncio loop this is actually safe because try_acquire is fully synchronous (no await between read and write), so no coroutine can interleave — but the lock is dead code and the documented invariant is false. If anyone ever adds an `await` inside try_acquire (e.g. an async hook emit before the increment), the unguarded read-modify-write becomes a real lost-update race on the concurrency cap.
original.claude_code_parity: N/A — internal accounting; the cap still holds today via cooperative scheduling.
original.proposed_fix: Either remove `_lock` and the misleading docstrings (state plainly that correctness relies on try_acquire being synchronous/await-free), or actually use the lock — but note an asyncio.Lock requires an async method, so the cleaner fix is to delete it and add a comment/assert that no await may be introduced into the acquire path.

title: Synthesis fallback adds up to 30s after max_wall_seconds, so the advertised hard wall-clock budget is exceeded on the success-but-empty path
proof: runner.py _drive_and_collect bounds ONLY the drain by max_wall_seconds: lines 429-432 `await asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.shield(drain_task), timeout=float(task.max_wall_seconds))`. AFTER that wait_for returns/times out, lines 474-493 run the v1.12.3 synthesis net `synth = await _synthesize_from_tool_results(...)` (line 480) on the condition `not output_text.strip() and executed_results and tool_executor is not None and state["finish_reason"] not in (FinishReason.TIMEOUT, FinishReason.ERROR)` (lines 474-478) — i.e. the success-but-empty-with-tools path. That synthesis is independently bounded by a SEPARATE budget: `_SYNTH_FALLBACK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS: float = 30.0` (line 510), enforced at line 569 `await asyncio.wait_for(_pump(), timeout=_SYNTH_FALLBACK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS)`. The schema advertised to the model reads "Hard wall-clock budget for the child" with `minimum: 1` and only an upper clamp (tool_wrapper.py:232-241), and _parse_args accepts any positive integer with no floor (tool_wrapper.py:871-873), so a child told it has e.g. 5s can run up to 5+30=35s. Crucially, no outer supervisor wait_for clamps this in the live path: agent_servicer.py:2044-2052 calls `dispatch_subagent_spawn(... supervisor_acquire=_acquire)`, where `_acquire` (agent_servicer.py:2634-2640) is only `sup.try_acquire` (a cap check), NOT `Supervisor.spawn_child`. dispatch_subagent_spawn -> _run_child_under_slot does `with slot_cm: result = await run_child(...)` (tool_wrapper.py:571-585) with no wait_for around run_child. Supervisor.spawn_child's own outer `asyncio.wait_for(coro, timeout=budget_s)` (supervisor.py:703) is therefore never invoked for spawns — a grep confirms spawn_child is not called anywhere in corlinman-server or corlinman-agent src. The background path (system/subagent/dispatcher.py:387 `await self._run_child_factory(req)` -> run_child) is likewise unwrapped. So nothing else clamps the elapsed time and total wall-clock = drain(<=max_wall_seconds) + synth(<=30s).
refined_fix: In runner.py _drive_and_collect, derive a single shared budget so synthesis cannot push total wall-clock past max_wall_seconds. Track drain elapsed (started_ms is already in scope at line 495) and pass the remainder as the synthesis timeout: at the synthesis call site (lines 480-488) compute `remaining = max(0.0, float(task.max_wall_seconds) - (_now_ms() - started_ms)/1000.0)` and pass `synth_timeout=min(_SYNTH_FALLBACK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, remaining)` into _synthesize_from_tool_results, replacing the hard-coded _SYNTH_FALLBACK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS at line 569 with that parameter (skip synthesis entirely if remaining <= 0). Alternatively (lower-effort, severity is low): document on the schema text at tool_wrapper.py:235 and on _SYNTH_FALLBACK_TIMEOUT_SECONDS (runner.py:507-510) that max_wall_seconds bounds the reasoning drain only and a fixed synthesis grace (mirroring the _TIMEOUT_GRACE_SECONDS convention at runner.py:369) may be added on top.
